Q1: I kept a pretty consistent study routine, honestly. I'd block out time each day, usually around 36 hours a week total, and just work through what needed to be done. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lectures while doing other things or record myself reading notes and play them back. It wasn't exciting, but it was systematic and it worked for me.
Q2: I tried to keep things regular because that's just how I operate best. I'd have set times during the week where I knew I'd be studying, though sometimes deadlines would force me to adjust a bit. Mostly though, I stuck to my schedule because deviating from it made me feel disorganized. Routine keeps the stress manageable for me.
Q3: Usually it was just part of my daily checklist—log in, see what's due, check for announcements. Sometimes it was because I needed to watch a video or check discussion posts. Honestly, it became pretty automatic, like checking email. I didn't always feel motivated to be there, but I showed up because that's what you do.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became pretty routine after the first few weeks. I'd just do it at the same time each day, usually in the morning with my coffee. It wasn't something I looked forward to, but it became a habit. Once something's a habit for me, I just keep doing it without thinking much about it.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd watch them, sometimes multiple times, and really absorb the content that way. I also found the discussion posts somewhat helpful because hearing other perspectives sometimes clarified things. The audio component of any resource was always what I gravitated toward.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the extra reading materials and some of the optional interactive stuff. It felt like busywork to me, and I was already putting in a lot of hours. If it wasn't going to directly help my grade or understanding, I didn't prioritize it. I guess I was efficient but maybe missed some depth because of that.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to exams or assignments. If it helped me understand something I'd be tested on, I was in. Also, if it was audio-based or something I could listen to, I was more likely to engage. Anything that felt like padding or filler just bored me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore, honestly. Required ones I did because they were required—simple as that. I didn't feel any internal pull toward optional stuff unless it seemed genuinely interesting, which was rare. I'm a complier by nature; tell me what to do and I'll do it, but don't expect me to go above and beyond.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them offline, especially audio files of lectures. I'd listen to them on my commute or while exercising. I also used my own notes extensively and sometimes looked up external videos that explained concepts better. A lot of my real studying happened away from the LMS.
Q10: Most of my review sessions wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd re-listen to downloaded lectures, go through my handwritten notes, or talk through concepts out loud to myself. That's actually how I learn best—through hearing and speaking—but none of that leaves a digital footprint. My engagement was real, just not always visible.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or audio file once and then use it repeatedly offline. I'd listen to the same material three or four times sometimes, especially before exams. The LMS would only show that single download, not the hours I spent actually engaging with that content afterward.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I felt bored and disconnected from the material. When the content got repetitive or I couldn't see the point of certain modules, my motivation dropped. The CBT sessions I had helped me manage my stress, but they didn't really fix the underlying boredom. I just pushed through because that's what I do.
Q13: Yeah, workload from other courses and extracurriculars definitely affected my LMS usage. When I was swamped, I'd do the bare minimum online and focus on what I knew would impact my grade most. Fatigue was real too—sometimes I'd log in but just skim through things without really processing. Competing responsibilities meant I had to prioritize ruthlessly.
Q14: Definitely. There were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. I was technically accessing content but not really absorbing it. Those sessions felt like going through the motions. I'd check the box mentally but know I'd need to revisit the material later when I could actually focus.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt tedious to me. I knew they mattered for participation, but writing out my thoughts felt like a chore compared to just listening and learning. I'd also delay anything that required a lot of reading. Eventually I'd get to it, but I definitely dragged my feet on certain things.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivator. I'd structure my whole week around what was due and when. Without deadlines, I probably would have spread things out differently or maybe even skipped some stuff. They kept me accountable, even when I wasn't feeling particularly interested in the material.
Q17: Feedback helped when it was specific and actionable. If I got good feedback, I felt validated in my approach and kept doing what I was doing. If it was critical, I'd adjust. But honestly, feedback came slowly sometimes, so by the time I got it, I'd already moved on to the next thing. It didn't always change my behavior in real-time.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, which worked for me because I like predictability. Having clear modules and deadlines meant I could plan my routine around them. I didn't love the structure, but I didn't fight it either. I just complied with it and moved through systematically.
Q19: The heavy reliance on video content made things easier for me as an auditory learner. What made it harder was when there were too many readings or text-heavy resources—I'd zone out. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced sometimes. If the design had more audio options throughout, I would have been more engaged.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need or getting confused by the interface. They could be clicking around a lot without finding what actually helps them. Or maybe they're like me sometimes—logged in but mentally checked out. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I didn't have super high LMS activity, but I performed well because I did a lot of studying offline. If you just looked at my login frequency or task completion, you'd think I wasn't trying. But my exam scores and assignment quality tell a different story. The LMS doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, but they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They definitely miss offline studying, re-reading downloaded materials, or any kind of auditory learning that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually processing and understanding the material, not just showing up. For me, it's about listening actively, thinking through concepts, and being able to apply what I've learned. It's not about how many times I logged in or how many posts I made. Real engagement is internal, not performative.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent listening to lectures offline or talking through concepts with myself. It wouldn't capture my stress levels or how bored I often felt despite doing the work. It also wouldn't show that I'm someone who complies with requirements but doesn't go beyond them unless there's a clear reason to. My engagement was steady but not enthusiastic, and that nuance gets lost in the data.
